data.title: IGA: Discount Widens Back Out, Making It A More Interesting Choice (Upgrade)
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The Voya Global Advantage and Premium Opportunity Fund (IGA) has seen its discount widen once again to a more attractive level. It has come closer to its longer-term average level but may also get assigned a higher valuation today relative to where it was ten years ago. This is because income-focused investors likely are willing to pay a relatively higher level given the higher distribution rate and monthly payout the fund has now put in place several years ago. That's one of the catalysts that can often drive a closed-end fund to see a different valuation being assigned.

IGA Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -2.53
  • Discount/Premium: -8.92%
  • Distribution Yield: 10.86%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.00%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $158 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

IGA's investment objective is "a high level of income; capital appreciation is secondary." They intend to write "call options on indexes or ETFs on an amount equal to approximately 50%-100% of the value of the Fund's common stock holdings."

Performance - Discount Widens Out Once Again

We last touched on IGA in September 2025, with a "Hold" rating based on the fund's narrowing discount at the time. Since that time, the fund has delivered some positive total returns, but nothing to get too excited about. That said, those returns would have looked a bit brighter just a month or so ago, before the Iran conflict put pressure on the broader equity market. The fund was also able to slightly outperform the S&P 500 Index during this period, thanks to its exposure being more value-oriented and also introducing some global exposure.

At one point, I held a long position in IGA. I noted that I sold out of the position in October 2024. I had noted that I didn't see anything specifically wrong with IGA, but I was simply looking to reduce the number of positions I held in my CEF portfolio. I ultimately sold the position and increased my position in BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust (BOE).

One of the main drivers was that the funds were quite similar and had historically had some pretty consistent correlation in terms of total returns. Since that time, that has continued to be the case, except that IGA has slightly edged BOE out on both total share price and NAV returns. Though the correlation still remains fairly high.

One area that is notable, though, is that during the April 2025 sell-off, IGA performed a fair bit better—with the latest broader equity pressures in the last month, we are seeing that play out once again.

Despite both of these funds operating with a call writing strategy, where they start to differ is when you dive into the underlying portfolio composition. BOE invests more heavily in information technology stocks, with IGA being more value-oriented and defensively positioned. Therefore, seeing the results we have where IGA dips to a lesser degree makes a lot of sense.

What I find more appealing about IGA, besides its differentiated portfolio bringing a bit of diversification, is the fund's valuation. The discount has once again widened back out a bit since our last focus piece. This has pushed the fund back out to its longer-term historical discount level.

With that said, there was a specific catalyst that likely prompted such a discount narrowing in the first place. That was the fund switching to a monthly distribution policy in 2024, and that came with a sizeable distribution increase at the time as well.

Distribution - Monthly Pay

The fund's current distribution is paid monthly at $0.085. This was previously a quarterly payout at $0.1970; thus, when they switched to the monthly distribution policy, it also came with them giving it a meaningful ~29.5% boost.

Income-focused investors tend to find monthly distributions more appealing. Further, they also tend to gravitate more toward higher distribution yields. Sometimes this can be to their own detriment, at least if they are also looking for capital preservation. IGA's current payout comes in at 10.86%, with the NAV rate at 9.89%. The higher the distribution rate, the more likely there will be NAV erosion. Not only does the fund need to produce around a 10% return to cover the payout, but they also have to earn the 1% expense ratio.

Since the fund boosted its payout, we've been in a solid period of growth. So in this period, we have seen the fund be able to not only pay out this attractively high distribution rate, but the NAV has not suffered at all. That's the good news, but they will continue to require the 10%+ returns annually to be able to continue to maintain the same going forward.

Below, we can see that the requirements to cover the distribution will primarily come from capital gains within the underlying portfolio or the options writing strategy. This is because the net investment income that is generated is only a relatively small portion of covering the payout. NII coverage as of the last semi-annual report would have come in at around 27.5%.

In terms of the tax classification of the distribution, we have often seen in recent years a combination of ordinary income, long-term capital gains, and/or return of capital characterizations. This often fluctuates quite a bit from year to year, so this varying mixture may give some investors pause who are more tax sensitive.

IGA's Portfolio

The turnover rate for this fund comes in at a fairly elevated level. In the last semi-annual report, it came in at 43% for that six-month period. In each of the prior two years, it was at 74%.

At the same time, we actually haven't seen too much in terms of a significant fluctuation in the overall sector allocation for the fund. Naturally, it does vary a bit, but it generally tends to put a significant weighting toward financials. This is then followed by industrials and healthcare—which is precisely what we see this time and what we also observed around six months ago in the breakdown.

The U.S. makes up the largest allocation for the fund, coming in at around 67%. That still does leave about a third of the fund to be invested elsewhere around the globe. This, too, is pretty much where the fund was positioned during our last update as well.

When looking at the top ten, we see that Alphabet (GOOGL) has slipped into the number one spot with an over 3% allocation. That replaced Meta Platforms (META) from the top spot, but that name remains, but has slipped to the third spot. Interestingly, GOOGL actually wasn't listed as a top holding previously, as it was too small a position in the fund. These are also the only two Magnificent 7 names being held in fairly meaningful weightings.

Aside from those two, we are presented with fairly unique top holding positions that aren't necessarily seen in too many other equity-focused funds. This is part of what sets IGA apart from some of its other call-writing peers, being a more value-oriented and globally focused fund.

Conclusion

With the market seeing some downside pressure more recently, that has made equities look a bit more appealing. As this fund is a more value-oriented and globally exposed choice, it makes it fairly unique. What makes it even more appealing at this time is that the fund's discount has widened fairly meaningfully since our prior update. That puts it closer to its longer-term average level, but that was primarily through a period where it was valued as a quarterly payer. With a more elevated distribution rate and a monthly payout schedule, a new, higher "normal" valuation could be something we see play out.

data.title: Meta Platforms: Muse Spark Launch Sends A Signal To Investors
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) is not the first big tech name that comes to my mind when I think of AI. It used to be Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), but of late, I have bet my money on Alphabet Inc. (GOOG). Nonetheless, I am a shareholder of all three of these companies. It's somewhat fascinating that I own these three stocks, given that I am primarily a small-cap investor. The most honest explanation is that I hardly miss an opportunity to invest in big tech names when they are presented to me at a discount.

Coming back to Meta, this is one of my core holdings. Unless things go horribly wrong for the company, I am not planning to book my gains. I'd rather let these gains accumulate and enjoy the dividends in the long run.

Yesterday (April 8), Meta launched Muse Spark, the first model in its Muse series. This comes as the first AI model launched since Alexandr Wang joined the company as its first Chief AI Officer in June 2025. Wang joined Meta after the tech giant acquired a 49% stake in Scale AI, the company he founded after dropping out of MIT.

I believe the launch of Muse Spark is intended to send an important signal to investors about the expected ROI from Meta's AI investments. My bullish thesis remains valid amid this new development, and I believe Meta deserves a place in my long-term portfolio at this valuation.

Muse Spark Marks MSL's First Tangible Product

Meta has been aggressively spending on AI. The company has integrated AI across some of its products, including the Advantage+ suite for advertisers, but Muse Spark is the first product to come out of Meta Superintelligence Labs, which was launched last year.

Muse Spark is a foundational model. So for now, comparing it with frontier models such as GPT is a futile task. This model is multimodal, meaning it can process text and also images.

Exhibit 1: Multimodal capabilities of Muse Spark

Upon testing Muse Spark, I noticed that this is a multi-agent system, as it has a Fast mode, which can be used to get immediate answers to questions, and a Thinking mode, which can be used to handle complex tasks such as math and science. You can access Muse Spark within the Meta AI app itself or by visiting the meta-ai website.

From a technical perspective, Muse Spark differs from the previous Llama series. This is a closed model, whereas Llama was launched as an open-source model. This might change in the future, but for now, Meta seems to be focusing on keeping the core components of Muse Spark private while MSL is developing larger, more advanced models.

I believe the launch of Muse Spark is aimed at signaling to investors that Meta has created a roadmap to improve ROI from its AI investments. Investors have been quite skeptical about Meta's aggressive AI investments, as the company, so far, has failed to launch meaningful AI products. Llama, the open-source model launched by Meta, has proven to be an underperforming model on several fronts. For instance, a study that compared the creative writing capabilities of different AI models found that Llama 4 had the worst performance.

Exhibit 2: Long-form creative writing benchmark scores for LLMs

Even when it comes to coding, which is arguably the most popular use case at a technical level, Llama 4 is shown to underperform its competitors.

Exhibit 3: Accuracy scores of specialized coding models

Meta needs to justify its AI investments. Llama 4 has been a disappointment. I believe this is where the Muse Spark launch fits in. By launching a closed AI model with multimodal capabilities, Meta has given hope to investors that it is on the right track to developing larger models with more advanced capabilities. Mr. Market has taken this news in stride, evidenced by the 7% increase in META stock on April 8.

Meta's AI Monetization Will Look Different From Established AI Giants

I am sure the majority of Seeking Alpha users have used one generative AI platform or the other. The monetization strategy of these GenAI tools is easy to understand. Pro or equivalent tiers have been introduced by the likes of ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, and Claude to monetize their users. That is just the beginning of the monetization process, and I am not planning to dive deep into their strategy.

For Meta, the path toward monetization looks different. Rather than using Muse Spark to drive traffic to Meta AI, the bigger goal is to create value for billions of people who use Meta products daily. This includes Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, Threads, and Ray-Ban glasses. In a blog post, Meta already hinted at how Muse Spark could integrate shopping tools to pull ideas directly from creators followed by a user. In the future, Muse Spark will be able to answer queries by incorporating Facebook and Instagram content directly into its response. Commenting on this, Meta wrote:

Meta AI can now help you discover what to wear, how to style a room, or what to buy for someone you know. Shopping mode draws from the styling inspiration and brand storytelling already happening across our apps, surfacing ideas from the creators and communities people already follow.

For users of Meta, Muse Spark is geared toward creating value to keep these users within Meta's ecosystem even when it comes to shopping.

For advertisers, nothing is more important than high-intent signals from consumers. This is where Muse Spark could play an important role. When Muse Spark is integrated into social media and IM platforms owned by Meta, users will use these AI capabilities for product discovery. Today, this traffic is usually captured by search engines. By capturing some of this traffic, Meta gains access to high-intent signals from users, which it can then feed to advertisers. For advertisers, this should create high ROI opportunities. I expect a future where advertisers would be able to place their products in posts/reels pushed to users via Muse Spark.

By keeping users locked into the Meta ecosystem, the company will be able to charge premium prices for its ad inventory. I believe Meta's AI monetization strategy will revolve around this theme.

In addition to this, I believe future versions of Muse Spark (or any other name Meta chooses to call the new models) will help the company gain traction with its ambitious conversational commerce efforts. Meta has been aggressively launching shopping features within its messaging apps, such as WhatsApp and Messenger. The company has already launched business AI assistants within these IM platforms. Paid messaging launched within WhatsApp has crossed the $2 billion annual revenue run rate mark as well. As Muse Spark is rolled out across IM platforms, I believe Meta's click-to-message ad revenue will surge in the long run. This is because native shopping features embedded into messaging apps will boost the ad inventory value for Meta and also increase the time users spend on these platforms.

My Bullish Thesis Stands

My bullish stance on Meta stems from a few factors.

  1. I believe Meta has a long runway to grow in international markets. North America still accounts for the bulk of revenue (thanks to high ARPUs) despite the user base being dominated by Asia-Pacific. I believe the contribution from these under-monetized international regions will improve in the coming years.
  2. WhatsApp monetization is still in early stages.
  3. Aggressive AI investments will eventually help Meta charge premium prices for its ad inventory.
  4. Meta is reasonably valued at a forward P/E of 20x (undervalued compared to its closest tech peers).

Risks

Meta's CapEx is expected to hit at least $115 billion this year. The primary driver of this CapEx growth is aggressive AI investments. So far, Meta has not been able to monetize its AI investments to an acceptable degree. A lot depends on the future models of Muse Spark. I am keeping a close eye on CapEx and ROI from these investments to determine if Meta's AI investments will face a similar fate as its metaverse investments.

I am cautious about the outlook for the global economy as well. The advertising industry is highly sensitive to macro shocks. If global growth slows down amid geopolitical tensions, Meta's revenue is likely to take a hit. I believe this threat is largely ignored by many investors.

Takeaway

Meta's Muse Spark introduction is expected to send a signal to investors that its AI investments are beginning to deliver results. Although Muse Spark, on its own, will not lead to any financial gains for now, I believe the company's strategy is to integrate future versions into its messaging and social media apps to increase the value proposition for both users and advertisers. I remain bullish on Meta, as I believe the company enjoys a long runway to grow.

data.title: Tesla: Waiting For 'Amazing Abundance' To Amaze
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) is seeing a familiar fan load up on shares following the release of its EV delivery report, which came in lighter than expected.

Cathie Wood’s ARK (ARKK, ARKX, ARKQ) just put +$28M into the stock, one that is currently sitting on YTD losses of over 20%. The investment also precedes TSLA’s full Q1 release set for April 22.

As an income-focused and value investor, I have never been bullish on TSLA. However, with shares trading at the midpoint of its 52-week range, I believe the name is worth a visit and at least some consideration, especially as its auto business further matures and as it sets its sights on the next leg of its growth story.

While I view shares as a Hold at current trading levels, I believe the outlook is bright and believe TSLA can ultimately seize upon its opportunities.

What Is "Amazing Abundance" And Why Is It Important To TSLA?

In my view, TSLA’s idea of “Amazing Abundance” is simply a vision for what the company wants to be when it grows up.

After nearly two decades of proving it can build electric cars at scale, the message now appears to be that the vehicle business was the foundation and not the ultimate endgame.

The recent delivery report tells part of that story. Deliveries rose modestly YOY but still missed expectations, marking one of TSLA’s weaker quarters in recent years. I don’t really view this in a negative way. To me, it just suggests a degree of maturity for the auto business.

A case in point is that competition from Chinese EV makers is intensifying, legacy automakers are pulling back on EV ambitions, and TSLA’s own lineup is narrowing as it winds down the Model S and Model X.

I’d say that it’s clear that the auto segment, which still makes up the bulk of revenue, is no longer the runaway growth engine it once was.

That’s where “Amazing Abundance” becomes important. TSLA appears to be positioning its future around autonomy, robotics, AI, and energy storage, areas where it believes its manufacturing scale and software expertise can converge.

Ultimately, the company is trying to persuade investors that Robotaxis, Optimus, and grid-scale batteries aren’t just side projects. Rather, they are being framed as the next leg of growth.

In my view, as the core auto business settles into a more competitive, slower-growth phase, the realization of this broader vision will become increasingly central to TSLA’s longer-term outlook.

What Is The Outlook For TSLA Stock?

Building off the idea of “Amazing Abundance,” TSLA’s outlook to me is based on a tale of two businesses. That is, a maturing auto segment that funds an ambitious push into autonomy, robotics, energy, and AI infrastructure.

There were some signs that the seeds were being planted in Q4. CAPEX, for example, is set to more than double to over +$20B as TSLA pours money into battery supply chains, AI compute, new factories for Cybercab, Semi, Megapack, and notably, an Optimus production line that will replace Model S and X capacity in Fremont.

To me, that’s a symbolic shift. TSLA is literally reallocating space from legacy vehicles to humanoid robots.

From an earnings standpoint, TSLA appears to have the capacity to focus in on their long-term vision. Automotive margins, for example, improved sequentially in Q4 despite lower deliveries.

In addition, FSD adoption crossed +1.1M paid users. While the transition toward subscriptions may pressure near-term margins, this should build recurring revenue. Energy, too, remains a high-growth and important variable, even as competition and tariffs loom.

At the end of the day, while the near-term financial optics may look messy, TSLA still appears to be positioning itself for what it believes is a much larger addressable market than vehicles alone.

For investors, the outlook from here is likely to be less on quarterly delivery beats and more on whether TSLA can execute on this capital-intensive pivot into autonomy, robotics, and energy at scale.

What To Watch When TSLA Reports Q1?

When TSLA reports Q1, the headline delivery miss will already be known. The more important story to me will be what sits underneath it and how Elon frames the quarter in the context of “Amazing Abundance.”

I believe investors should definitely watch automotive margins. In Q4, margins improved sequentially despite lower deliveries, largely due to mix and operational efficiency.

If TSLA can show that profitability is holding up even as volumes wobble and competition intensifies, it would likely support the idea that the auto business is becoming a stable cash engine rather than a pure growth story.

Second, keep an eye on FSD adoption and commentary around the shift to a subscription model. With over a million paid users and early robotaxi activity underway in Austin, updates here would help bridge today’s vehicle sales to tomorrow’s autonomy-driven revenue model.

Third, energy deployments will likely matter more than usual. Any stabilization or reacceleration after the recent dip would support TSLA’s claim that energy storage and solar are key pillars of the next growth phase.

Finally, listen closely for updates on Cybercab, Optimus, and CAPEX pacing. I view progress, timelines, and factory buildouts as tangible markers of whether Tesla’s ambitious pivot from cars to AI, robotics, and autonomy is moving beyond merely the vision phase.

Is TSLA Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At current levels, I view TSLA as a Hold.

My rating may sound underwhelming for a company talking about robotaxis, humanoid robots, and “Amazing Abundance,” but I think it’s the best way to look at the stock today.

In my view, the auto business is clearly maturing. Deliveries are no longer consistently blowing past expectations, competition is rising, and the lineup is getting leaner. That part of the story looks more like a steady, capital-generating operation than a hypergrowth engine.

At the same time, the next leg of TSLA’s story is still very much in the buildout phase. Billions are being poured into factories, AI infrastructure, battery supply chains, and entirely new product categories that are not yet contributing meaningfully to revenue.

Robotaxis, Optimus, and expanded energy deployments all sound compelling, but they are still more vision than financial reality.

That’s why I don’t view the stock as a Buy here, but I also can’t justify calling it a Sell. TSLA has a history of solving hard problems and turning skepticism into opportunity, and I’m willing to give them the benefit of the doubt at this point.

For now, I think TSLA is a name worth watching closely. If “Amazing Abundance” starts to show up in tangible results, and that’s a big "IF," today’s midpoint pricing could look far more interesting, in my view.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Retreats
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks decreased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment increased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 2.2 percentage points to 35.7%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the eighth consecutive week.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 6.3 percentage points to 21.3%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 90th time in 92 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 8.4 percentage points to 43.0%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the ninth consecutive week.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 10.6 percentage points to –7.2%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the ninth consecutive week.

This week’s special question asked AAII members what changes, if any, they’ve made to their portfolio since the start of the Iran war.

Here is how they responded:

  • I have not made any changes (maintained existing positions): 42.3%.
  • I have added to existing positions or bought new ones at lower prices: 24.9%.
  • I have reduced equity exposure (sold stocks or funds, raised cash): 14.8%.
  • I have made other adjustments (e.g., rebalanced, increased bond or commodity allocation): 10.6%.
  • I have shifted holdings toward defensive sectors (e.g., energy, defense, utilities): 7.4%.

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 35.7%, up 2.2 percentage points.
  • Neutral: 21.3%, up 6.3 percentage points.
  • Bearish: 43.0%, down 8.4 percentage points.

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%.
  • Neutral: 31.5%.
  • Bearish: 31.0%.

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: British American Tobacco: The New Categories' Opportunity And Challenge
data.description:
Uncertain times are good for defensive stocks, and 2026 isn't turning out to be any different. Even as the S&P 500 (SP500) is down by 1% YTD, British American Tobacco p.l.c. (NYSE:BTI) (OTCPK:BTAFF) or BAT is up by ~6%.

The company's fundamentals back the rise and indicate that the uptick can continue. While risks exist too, here I discuss why the upside is still greater.

New Categories' Segment Expands Further...

Second only to Philip Morris (PM) in its expansion of smoke-free products, BAT continues to see encouraging trends from its new categories segment, which is the highlight for the company right now. Here are the key positive developments from 2025 for the segment, which comprises vapes, smokeless tobacco sticks, and nicotine pouches:

  • Bigger Revenue Impact, Bolstered By Strong H2 2025 Performance

It brought in 14.1% of total revenue during the year, up from 13.3% in 2024. Further, the category's revenue grew by 7% in the year, which is slightly better than the expectation of "mid-single-digit growth". It does bear mentioning, though, that the growth rate slowed down from 8.9% in 2024.

Still, the numbers for H2 2025 are encouraging, with an 8.5% YoY increase seen during this time compared to just a 2.3% YoY increase in H1 2025. This is an important development, since new product launches were due in H2 2025, as I noted in the last article in the stock.

It launched products like the vape Vuse Ultra, heated tobacco stick glo Hilo, and nicotine pouch Velo Shift. The company says they have seen "encouraging early results" and have "further targeted rollouts planned in 2026."

  • Growing Profitability

The segment is also witnessing improved profitability. Category contribution, a non-GAAP measure defined as revenue minus variable costs, remained positive for the second consecutive year. And even saw a robust 77% YoY increase. The category contribution margin also expanded, to 12%, from 7.3% in 2024.

...But Signs Of Weakness Are Evident

Modern oral nicotine pouches, which bring in 31% of the new categories' revenues, were the standout new categories' sub-segment last year. It saw a robust growth of 48%, which gets particularly highlighted in the context of the company's challenges in the vapor, or vaping, sub-segment.

Vapor, which is the biggest contributor to new categories' revenue at 43%, saw a significant 10.8% revenue drop in 2025. This is not just the second consecutive year of a drop in the segment's number in 2024; it's also an acceleration in the rate of decline from 2.5% then. Making matters worse is the fact that the drop is visible across geographies, with details as follows:

  • The US market, which contributes to 61% of the revenue from vapor, saw a 3.4% drop in 2025.
  • Europe and the Americas, the biggest market for new categories, bringing in half the segment's revenues last year, saw an even bigger 11.4% drop for vapor.
  • While Asia-Pacific, the Middle East, and Africa (APMEA) bring less than 5% of vapor revenues, the market noted the biggest drop of 39.4%.

The company attributes the overall decline to "the continued impact of illicit products, mainly in the U.S. and Canada, and regulatory and excise changes in the UK, Poland, and France, and market exits." Vapes have faced regulatory challenges for years now, primarily owing to their health impact, and more recently have become a target of unauthorized vape substitutes.

Positive Outlook...

Still, there's plenty of comfort to be found from the company's 2026 outlook, which is supported by BAT's performance in 2025. The company expects key growth rates this year to be towards the lower end of the medium-term guidance, as in the slide below.

Even if revenues grow by 3%, though, they would still be an improvement over the 2.1% increase seen in 2025. In fact, even in the past year, the number was a shade higher than the company's projection of 1-2%. This growth is expected to be supported by "double-digit New Category growth", which would mark an improvement over the 7% increase noted earlier, especially after the segment cooled off from 2024.

It's also encouraging that new categories could support profitability this year as well. And even otherwise, a 4% increase in adjusted operating profit would be an acceleration (2025: 2.3%), much like the 5% adjusted diluted EPS growth figure (2025: 3.4%).

...Supports Market Multiples

Assuming that the adjusted EPS grows by 5% this year, BTI's forward non-GAAP P/E comes to 12.1x, which is higher than the stock's five-year average of 8.8x for sure. But it's still not bad at all for two reasons:

1. Case For Premium: BAT's progress in new categories merits some premium. Compare it with Altria (MO), which has made precious little progress in this regard and still trades at a forward P/E of 11.9x, barely lower than BTI.

2. Favorable Sector Comparison: The five big tobacco stocks, PM, MO, BTI, Japan Tobacco (OTCPK:JAPAY), and Imperial Brands (OTCPK:IMBBY), have an average forward P/E of 14.3x, indicating that BTI can still rise by some 15-20%. Its ratio sits right in the middle of the tobacco set, with PM and JAPAY at higher valuations of over 19x each and MO and IMBBY at 11.9x and 9.4x, respectively.

Over the medium term (2029), the stock's forward P/E falls further to 10x. This makes it better placed compared with all tobacco peers, save IMBBY. As per analysts' estimates on Seeking Alpha, PM, MO, and IMBBY trade at P/Es of 11x, 11.1x, and 7.4x, respectively, for 2029. The figures for JAPAY aren't available, but assuming that its profits grow at the past five years' CAGR of 10.5%, its forward P/E would also be at a higher 14.1x.

Don't Forget The Dividends

Besides the potential price upside, a big plus from an investment in BTI is the dividend yield. At 5.7%, its forward yield is second only to MO's at 6.35%. After increasing dividends by 2% last year, the company aims to maintain progressive dividends.

The stock's payout ratio at 69.4% in 2025 is high, but then it's no higher than it has been in the past years. And it has consistently paid them for the past 27 years.

What's Next?

Dividends offer predictable returns in what could be a volatile year for the stock markets. Just for this, there's reason to consider BTI. Especially as there isn't any real risk of a price crash for the consumer staple stock right now.

Beyond 2026, the real highlight for a medium- to long-term investment in the stock is its growing new categories business, despite the challenges to its vaping market. BAT's financial growth prospects are also encouraging. I'm maintaining a Buy rating on BTI.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AbbVie: Buy Or Sell Ahead Of Q1 Earnings? It's A Buy, But With Caveats (Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Overview

AbbVie Inc. (ABBV), the North Chicago, Illinois-based Pharma giant, is expected to report its Q1 2026 earnings around April 29th - in just under three weeks' time.

A few days ago, AbbVie released an 8-K filing in which it stated:

Reported GAAP earnings and adjusted non-GAAP earnings for the first quarter of 2026 are expected to include acquired IPR&D and milestones expense of $744 million on a pre-tax basis, representing an unfavorable impact of $0.41 to both GAAP diluted earnings per share and adjusted non-GAAP diluted earnings per share.

AbbVie provided very clear guidance for both Q1 and full-year 2026 revenues and earnings on its Q4 2025 earnings call - in Q1, for example, the Pharma forecast for revenues of $14.7bn, with its immunology division contributing $7.1bn, Neuroscience division $2.8bn, Oncology, $1.6bn, and aesthetics revenues of $1.2bn. Eye care products and older assets such as Mavyret (hepatitis), Creon (pancreatic insufficiency), and Linzess (irritable bowel syndrome) comprise the remaining ~$2bn of revenues.

Adjusted earnings per share ("EPS") were expected to be between $2.97 and $3.01, but in line with AbbVie's downgraded guidance, analysts are now expecting the company to report a normalized EPS figure of $2.93 and a GAAP figure of $1.43.

All told, on a constant currency basis, if AbbVie meets its revenue guidance, it will represent an annual uplift (on a constant currency basis) of ~8% and an uplift in EPS of nearly 100%, while across the whole of 2026, AbbVie is forecasting revenues of ~$67bn, up 9.5%, and adjusted EPS to be $14.37 - $14.57, let's say $14 accounting for the IPRD charges, which is a 40% uplift.

My research suggests that among U.S. and European "Big Pharma" companies, only Eli Lilly (LLY), the world's largest pharmaceutical by market cap, can match AbbVie's rate of growth (thanks almost exclusively to Lilly's weight loss and Type 2 diabetes drugs Zepbound/Mounjaro) in 2026, yet year-to-date, AbbVie stock has fallen by ~8%.

In my last note on the company in early February, I speculated about my own holding of AbbVie stock, which I have held for nearly five years and which has doubled in value, and whether it may be time to cash in my gains. I noted that:

Tinkering with an investment portfolio can often backfire - a patient investor is a successful investor - but in AbbVie's case, I wonder if there are genuine prospects for share price growth in 2026, or if, had I exited my position yesterday, I might have invested it more successfully elsewhere.

AbbVie ultimately downgraded its 2025 guidance, albeit, similar to this quarter, it was a minor downgrade to EPS of ~$0.42, related to an IPR&D charge, and management has promised high single-digit revenue CAGR through to the end of the decade.

AbbVie stock has done little but gain in value ever since it was spun out of Abbott Laboratories in 2012 - stock is up ~470% since - but in this post, I am going to take a closer look at the longer-term outlook and try to establish a "fair value" price for the stock to see if the long-term "buy and hold" thesis still holds.

AbbVie Products & Pipeline Overview - Immunology & Oncology

Let's begin by analyzing what I believe are, and will continue to be, AbbVie's two most important divisions well into the next decade - immunology and oncology.

In the table above, I have included all of AbbVie's existing products and several pipeline assets I expect to be commercialized in the coming years. I have included year of approval, mechanism of action ("MoA"), indication, revenues in the years 2023-2025, and projected revenues to 2030.

A first, important note to make is that after the patent expiry of its biggest-selling asset, the immunology drug Humira (which earned >$23bn in 2022), AbbVie has no major upcoming patent expiries, meaning products command high prices and are not subject to generic competition - great news for AbbVie's bottom line.

Starting with Immunology, we can see how impressively AbbVie dealt with Humira's loss of exclusivity ("LOE"). While division revenues did fall in 2023, from $28.9bn to $26.1bn, in 2025 the division earned >$30bn, and in 2026 it will grow by ~13%, AbbVie forecasts, accounting for >50% of total revenues.

Skyrizi and Rinvoq have demonstrated stunning growth, covering off all the lost Humira revenues and securing approvals in nearly all of Humira's indications - plaque psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, Crohn's disease, and ulcerative colitis for Skyrizi, and rheumatoid arthritis, atopic dermatitis, ankylosing spondylitis, and more for Rinvoq.

AbbVie is forecasting for >20% revenue growth for Skyrizi and Rinvoq in 2026, which is remarkable, but these are highly competitive markets (not surprising, given how lucrative they are) - think Novartis' (NVS) Cosentyx, Sanofi's (SNY) Dupixent, Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ) recently approved Icotyde, Eli Lilly's Ebglyss, plus countless pipeline assets - Amgen's B-cell targeting rocatinlimab, Vor Biopharma's (VOR) telitacicept, for example, and likely more than one hundred others being developed by aspiring biotechs.

As we can see above, AbbVie's reliance on Skyrizi and Rinvoq is also quite high - the only late-stage immunology pipeline asset AbbVie has (as far as I am aware) is lutikizumab, indicated for hidradenitis suppurativa, and I see this drug's revenue contribution in 2030 as marginal. This could be a concern, as Skyrizi patents may expire around 2033.

In terms of pipeline, AbbVie appears to have been more focused on oncology. As we can see, this is a division that is not showing much growth, as Imbruvica's revenues are likely now in terminal decline (the drug is subject to price controls as a result of the Inflation Reduction Act).

AbbVie has invested heavily in antibody drug conjugates ("biological missiles" composed of a monoclonal antibody, or mAbs, covalently attached to a cytotoxic drug via a chemical linker), paying ~$6bn to acquire Stemcentryx in 2016 and ~$10bn to buy out Immunogen in 2023.

As we can see in the table above, I have modeled for Oncology becoming a ~$14bn revenue division by 2030, driven by growth for recently approved Elahere, an ADC; Epkinly, a heme cancer drug; and most recently approved - last year - Emrelis, in lung cancer, plus three new product approvals - Temab-A, in colorectal cancer; Pivekimab Sunirine; and another heme cancer drug, Etentamig.

As a warning, however, I have also included navitoclax - once considered by AbbVie to be a multi-"blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) selling cancer drug in waiting in indications such as myelofibrosis, high levels of patient withdrawals have cast doubt over whether it will make it to market at all. I have assigned no revenues to this drug, and we must acknowledge that any pipeline drug, even in late-stage studies, carries a risk that approval will not be granted (or another Pharma may develop a superior drug).

In summary, AbbVie appears to be making big bets in oncology - a field where it is less dominant than in immunology, with only one late-stage immunology pipeline asset. Should its bet on ADC drugs not prove successful, come 2033, the Pharma may be facing the patent expiry of Skyrizi, but replacing that drug's declining sales may prove trickier than offsetting Humira's declining sales post-LOE was in 2023.

AbbVie: Products & Pipeline Overview - Aesthetics, Neuroscience, Others

Now, let's take a look at aesthetics, neuroscience, and other products.

AbbVie's >$60bn acquisition of Allergan in 2020 gave it access to Botox, which has proven to be a successful cash cow for the company, generating >$10bn in revenues since 2023 as an aesthetic treatment and >$14bn as a migraine treatment. While competition is intense and focused on longer-acting therapies, I model Botox revenues to keep growing and note the potential approval of trenibotulinumtoxinE this year or next. In short, I model for the ongoing growth of this division - CAGR ~11%.

Meanwhile, AbbVie has made bullish projections for its migraine meds Ubrelvy and Qulipta in 2026 and for its depression med Vraylar. As such, I model for CAGR growth of ~8% for neuroscience between 2025 and 2030 for the division as a whole and expect tavapadon to be approved for Parkinson's, plus potentially an Alzheimer's therapy before 2030, although we should note the exceptionally high failure rate of Alzheimer's drugs to date.

In eye disease, Reproxalap, a drug AbbVie has partnered with Aldeyra Therapeutics (ALDX), was recently rejected for approval in the U.S. for the third time. It could be the end of the road for this asset, then; however, I model for a modest $300m of revenues by 2030 while awaiting updates. RGX-314, a gene therapy, has a Biologics License Application under FDA review and could soon be challenging the likes of Regeneron's (REGN) Eylea and Roche's (RHHBY) for market share - I model for ~$1.5bn of revenues by 2030.

Discounted Cash Flow Analysis

Now, let's plug these revenues into a basic forward-looking income statement as shown below:

This is a "broad brush" table (EPS likely falls somewhere between GAAP and non-GAAP), but as we can see, my assumption is that AbbVie can be an ~$85bn revenue pharma company by 2030, delivering operating income of >$22bn. The long-term (to 2030) forward price-to-sales ("P/S") ratio is therefore ~4.4x, based on AbbVie's current market cap valuation of $375bn at the time of writing, and price to operating income would be around ~17x.

There are, of course, countless variables to consider - for example, new drugs that are making ~$1bn of revenues in 2030 could go on to earn >$10bn by the mid-2030s. Some drugs won't make it to market at all due to efficacy or safety concerns. AbbVie could also make strategic M&A deals (although its long-term debt currently stands at an eye-watering $58.9bn, with >$6bn of short-term debt).

However, the one concern I'd highlight above all others is the bets AbbVie is making in oncology and the question mark over whether, when Skyrizi and Rinvoq patents are threatened in the 2030s, the Pharma will be able to overcome these patent cliffs with the same aplomb as in 2023, when Humira lost patent protection.

In terms of a "fair value" price for AbbVie stock, I'll finally share some ballpark DCF modeling for AbbVie below:

As we can see, my "fair value" price for AbbVie stock today comes to $233, an almost 10% premium to the current traded price.

All things considered, then, I don't think it's time to sell AbbVie stock yet - I'd advise readers to buy and benefit from a 3.3% dividend yield - but an overview of products and pipeline does provide us with some interesting questions about what the company may look like in five years' time.

I am broadly satisfied management can fulfill its high single-digit CAGR revenue forecasts to 2030, and therefore I won't be selling ahead of Q1 earnings - and I'd advise readers to listen carefully for any news on AbbVie's weight loss candidate ABBV-295, a long-acting amylin analog.

In a year's time, however, I suspect I will be asking the same questions again and seeking clarity over long-term plans for the immunology division, which has always been the jewel in this Pharma's crown and will likely need to continue to be so if the Pharma is not to suffer a downward price correction long-term. By Q1 27, we may have reached "peak AbbVie," but I don't think we have yet.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Endava Struggles As AI Pivot Pressures Margins, Growth Remains Elusive
data.description:
Investment Outlook

Endava plc (DAVA) reported FQ2 2026 financial results, with the stock continuing to fall thereafter on negative investor sentiment due to negative earnings.

I previously analyzed DAVA in August 2025 with a Sell outlook due to a poor employee utilization rate amid slowing client demand.

Client demand remains slow in certain industries while the company continues to invest in GenAI initiatives internally and through its Dava.Flow platform and extensions; earnings continue to worsen on sliding YoY revenue.

DAVA will have to become more efficient to improve earnings, likely through a headcount reduction.

Until then, my outlook on DAVA remains a bearish Sell.

Endava’s Market And Approach

The firm provides software, technology engineering, and related consulting services to organizations of all sizes worldwide.

The pie charts below illustrate the revenue contribution by client industry and by region, as indicated:

A Grand View Research market research report estimated that the worldwide software engineering and IT consulting market was $273 billion in 2022 and was projected to exceed $678 billion by 2030.

If achieved, this growth rate would be a CAGR in excess of 12% from 2023 to 2030, a fairly strong rate of growth.

Organizations of all sizes are seeking to transition from their closed, legacy systems to cloud-based environments and have a growing desire to modernize their IT operations in the process.

Also, many companies are contemplating or piloting GenAI systems to reduce costs and increase efficiencies, especially in low complexity business functions such as simple customer service and procurement processes.

According to Grand View, the chart below shows the historical and projected future growth trajectory for the U.S. Software Consulting Market through 2030, by application type:

The company competes with various industry players, such as consulting companies, information technology providers, as well as ‘in-house’ engineering by companies.

Competitors in the market for software consulting include the following players:

  • Accenture (ACN)

  • Atos SE

  • Capgemini (CAPMF)

  • CGI Group

  • Clearfind

  • Cognizant (CTSH)

  • Deloitte Touche Tohmatsu Ltd.

  • Ernst & Young LLP

  • International Business Machines (IBM)

  • Oracle Corp. (ORCL)

  • PricewaterhouseCoopers B.V.

  • Rapport IT

  • SAP SE (SAP)

  • Trianz

Endava is a mid-cap specialist player seeking to take advantage of rising enterprise GenAI budgets by packaging methodology and partner ecosystems to capture outcome-based engagement opportunities versus headcount-based projects.

Recent Financial Trends

Topline revenue rose sequentially but was down 5.9% year-over-year due to client losses and Payments, Mobility and TMT industry weakness.

Endava’s core operating leverage has moved above 100% in the most recent quarter on improved SG&A cost control.

Gross profit margin (blue line) worsened both sequentially and year-over-year due to lower headcount utilization and, therefore, weaker revenue per delivery employee. FX was also a gross margin headwind. SG&A costs fell 1% year-over-year in constant currency.

Earnings per share remained heavily negative due to the firm’s pivot to AI-native delivery, pressuring gross margins and increasing borrowings to help finance the company's share buyback efforts.

A table of various forward and trailing twelve-month metrics is shown here:

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

Capital Allocation

As of December 31, 2025, Endava had $92 million in cash and equivalents and $273 million in long-term debt.

Free cash flow for the trailing twelve-month period ended Dec. 31, 2025, was $57.3 million after $19.2 million of capital expenditures.

However, stock-based compensation [SBC] for the period was $32.7 million, leaving free cash flow net of SBC at only $24.6 million.

SBC as a percentage of revenue was 3.3% in 2025, somewhat elevated by IT consulting firm averages.

Stock repurchases were significant at a net of $121 million, or about 2.1x free cash flow and nearly 5x FCF net of SBC.

But borrowings rose to fund the repurchase program, so the current level of stock buybacks is not sustainable forever.

Why I’m Bearish On Endava

Endava is experiencing a number of revenue challenges from weak client spending, FX headwinds and margin pressure due to the continued need for GenAI investments.

The firm is also reducing headcount in soft-demand areas while reskilling its remaining employees for the transition to GenAI engagements.

So, topline revenue growth is lacking, and earnings have remained sharply negative.

This is not uncommon in the wider consulting industry, which has struggled to retain gains after the pandemic period headcount expansion while hoping for a quick transition to GenAI engagements.

Those engagements have mostly been pilot projects for the low-hanging fruit cost-takeout areas like basic customer service and procurement functions.

Many of those pilot projects have not moved forward into production, as companies have sought to make incremental changes with new technologies that are themselves evolving at a rapid pace, with new GenAI models appearing twice per year or faster.

The chart below shows the frequency of a number of keyphrases or words that appeared in the most recent management conference call with analysts.

The graphic shows that the company has seen FX headwinds and a challenging and complicated enterprise market for implementing agentic AI capabilities.

Looking ahead, the company expects further earnings pressure from ongoing investments in GenAI capabilities, but increased outcome-based work from AI engagements.

With its Dava.Flow flagship offering and adoption of ChatGPT Enterprise internally, management is continuing to invest in GenAI internally across the organization, and externally by trying to replicate its success with the Payments vertical in Insurance, Healthcare and other complex workflows dominated by regulatory requirements.

The table highlights a very low forward EV/Sales multiple, which is not surprising given its barely-positive forward growth rate expectation.

The market is assigning a lower EV/EBITDA multiple, likely due to its weak operating outlook.

So, in my view, Endava is in the middle of a painful and probably lengthy transition toward a GenAI-centric approach to its primary engagement focus.

DAVA’s stock price has fallen over 75% in the past year, while it seems management is continuing to operate in incremental moves as shareholder value is being destroyed.

Until the company can show improved earnings with a more efficient headcount, my outlook remains a Sell on DAVA.

data.title: Behind Seeking Alpha Quant Featuring Steven Cress
data.description:
Follow Steven Cress on Seeking Alpha

Get Started With Alpha Picks

Join Pro Quant Portfolio Now!

Sign Up For Premium Today!

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Daniel Snyder: Hello everyone. I'm Daniel Snyder from Seeking Alpha. Thank you so much for taking the time to hang out with us here today. We are diving into the world of the Quant system with none other than Steven Cress. All the questions that you have ever had about factor grades, dividend grades, or REIT grades, we are diving into it here today. But before we dive into conversation with him and get the back history of the Quant system or what is the Quant system and how does it work and how does it refresh and all of that great data, let’s go ahead and get a quick legal disclaimer out of the way here.

We are not advising you personally concerning the nature, potential, value, or suitability of any particular security. You alone are solely responsible for determining whether any investment, security, strategy, or any product or service is appropriate or suitable for you based on your investment objectives and personal and financial situation.

This presentation is for information purposes only. Content is presented as of the date published or indicated and may be superseded by future events. It represents my opinions and Steven Cress' opinions, which may not reflect the views of Seeking Alpha as a whole.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, US investment adviser, or investment bank.

And now with that out of the way, we're going to dive into the man, the myth, the legend, Steven Cress, who, I mean, the mastermind behind the Seeking Alpha Quant. Let's dive into the history a little bit before we dive into the platform today, Steve.

Lots going on, lots to talk about, especially data side of things, but the history of you and Seeking Alpha, because you actually developed this Quant system before Seeking Alpha acquired your company, CressCap, back in 2018, if I remember correctly. What's the prior history of that? When did you get into the world of Quant? How did you leverage Quant in your previous, I know you were at Stanley for a while. Why don't you just go ahead and share a little bit of that information with everybody today?

Steven Cress: Yeah. Absolutely. Well, first, thank you very much for organizing this today, and I think the timing is perfect for investors and potential investors to be looking at the market and looking at the Quant system. I've heard a number of talking heads on TV today saying, hey, this could really be the market bottom or close to the market bottom. So, absolutely terrific period to start looking at stocks.

In terms of my history, I've been on Wall Street for almost 40 years. 11 of those years, actually, I spent in the City of London working in finance as well. And my time in Quant really dates back to almost 2000. Shortly after the tech bubble, I started to devise systems for my clients when I worked at Morgan Stanley that were very quantitative oriented.

I worked in institutional sales at the time, selling equity research from our analysts to large institutional investors. And during the TMT era, a lot of the stocks, a lot of the banking deals really started to go belly up. So, clients were saying, hey, we need a better system than just investing in tech stocks. What do you have?

So, I started to devise my own Quant models at that period. And lo and behold, those models worked really well. Ended up spending about 13 years at Morgan Stanley and eventually ran a proprietary trading desk in quantitative strategies. So that was really the beginning of it, and necessity is the mother of invention. Stocks weren't working at the time. There were a lot of fears in the market. So, I wanted to develop a system where we could really cut through the noise and just look at companies that had really good fundamentals. So, I started to create the Quant system really way back then, so almost 26 years ago.

DS: And so, has it evolved over the years, or is it still the original Quant system that you developed all those years ago?

SC: No. It absolutely evolves. I don't want to say every day, but the beauty of Quant is it's like searching for the Holy Grail. You're constantly out there. You're constantly trying to improve your methods, improve your parameters, your criteria. So, we do constantly tweak the Quant model, especially products that we create that are based on the Quant model. A lot of the Quant system is very similar to what we established here at Seeking Alpha back in, I want to say, around 2018.

So, the essence of the core model is the same, but we've developed products off of it, Dividend Safety Grades, Alpha Picks as a portfolio product, the PRO Quant Portfolio as a product, ETF grades, REIT grades, and all those tend to have some metrics and parameters and criteria that are a little bit different. And especially with the portfolio of products, we really back-test those factors that we assess in trying to get the best performance that we possibly can.

DS: Yeah. Now, we're going to dive into as much as we can here today. Obviously, there is some proprietary information within the Quant system that we won't be able to answer, but everyone that – you're turning into this video live with us, if you have questions, make sure you're dropping them in the chat. We're going to try to get to questions here after a little bit of a presentation walking through the platform and the data behind the Quant system and the back-testing and everything like that.

So, Steve, why don't we go ahead and jump in? I think we should go to the platform. I think, I mean, everybody, whether they're new or they're familiar with Seeking Alpha, you see the Quant system on every single Symbol page. You see it all across the site, but today, specifically, we're going to highlight the factor grades, the dividend grades, and the REIT grades. And then you have another webinar later this month where you're going to be joining Fred from the Product team and be talking about the screeners and portfolio health check and some of the other things we have on the site. But to get things off, maybe we can dive into, like, the Quant Grades, right, and the trifecta.

SC: Yeah. So, I thought what I would do is, I want to highlight to people where they can get more information. So, after this webinar today, they'll be able to find a lot of this on their own. So, if you look on the right hand side, this is a stock page or you can refer to it as a symbol page. It's really the core to the Seeking Alpha Premium. It pulls a lot of different information together. You can find contributor articles over on the left hand side. You can find news from our incredible news team. And then the right side, you find the Quant grades.

So, what I want to start with is, if you see there's a question mark over here on factor grades for the Quant System, and you'll see there's a little snippet of information. If you click on the Quant ranking of factor grades, that will actually bring you to an article that I wrote. And this will help provide you with a little bit of how the factor grades work. And I'm just going to walk you through this right now. So, what are Quant ratings and what are factor grades?

Well, at Seeking Alpha, I often tell people the Quant system is very similar to what a fundamental analyst does. An analyst that works at Morgan Stanley or Goldman Sachs or Merrill Lynch, they are fundamental investment research analyst. And they look at core investment characteristics such as value and growth and profitability. Our Quant system is really very similar where we look at the same type of investment characteristics that an analyst looks at.

The only difference is, as a human being, as an analyst, having been an analyst myself, when you cover a number of companies and you have to write research on those companies, you're really only capable of maybe covering 15 to 20 stocks within a given sector. And when you write research on those particular stocks, maybe you can write research, a good large report, once a month and then just update a lot of information. So, it's fairly infrequently you get those written research reports.

The beauty of the Quant system is that we look at the same investment characteristics, but we upload data from cash flow statements, income statements, balance sheets, and hundreds of different financial metrics every single day. And when we upload that, we take those metrics and those data points for a company, and we compare it to all the other companies in this sector. And this allows us to rank the best companies from the weakest companies. And in performing that ranking, we could actually assign a stock a Strong Buy, a Buy, a Hold, Sell, or a Strong Sell based on where it falls.

So, the real difference is, we have the ability to basically assess and provide a directional recommendation on thousands and thousands of stocks on a daily basis, where most conventional analysts can just do a couple updates every month on a particular stock. So, you're getting near real time information in terms of that directional recommendation, and we have a really good track record. So, that is the first thing I want to describe. What makes Quant different than a fundamental analyst? It's basically taking the same investment characteristics, but adding computer processing, putting it on steroids, and being able to generate large amounts of that data every single day that point to those recommendations.

So, scroll down. You'll see how is this useful for investors. Well, it's very useful for investors. We have portfolio tools where you could load up your portfolio onto the platform so you could – every single day, it gives you the ability to get a fresh recommendation on the stocks that you own. So, you'll see the overall rating. Is it a Buy, is it a Sell, or is it a Hold? But in addition, when you look at those factors such as value, growth, profitability, you'll be able to see on those individual metrics where they stand versus the rest of the sector. You get an instant characterization.

So, I designed this system. I've been working on it basically since the year 2000. I actually created my own company. I had a hedge fund for a couple of years that was based on Quant strategies. And when I was running the hedge fund, I also developed a fintech company that Seeking Alpha liked so much they bought the company, and that's what has brought me to Seeking Alpha. I don't think I added that before when I was talking about my history. So, probably an important point there. That's how I joined Seeking Alpha. They purchased my company.

So, back to how our Quant system works, which stocks are covered and which aren't? We cover just about all stocks in the United States and ADRs that trade in the United States as well. And when we look at a company we do look at historical data metrics but we also look at forward data metrics as well. So, when we look at forward data metrics, that means we're looking at revenue growth, EPS growth, EBITDA growth. And what we do is we take the consensus of Wall Street analyst estimates when we're looking at that growth. Companies that we do not cover often will find that there's no Wall Street coverage. So, if there's no Wall Street coverage on a company, we will not cover it in our Quant system.

We also do not cover stocks that trade on foreign exchanges. If there's an ADR, American Depository Receipt, and that trades in the U.S., we will cover it. But if it purely trades on a foreign exchange, we do not cover it. So, that's a little bit of information about the factor grades. What I really want to do is, take you to a specific stock so I can walk you through how it actually works. This stock is called Celestica. It's a name that we've liked for quite a long time, and you can actually see how long that we've liked it. There is a Ratings tab, and we're very transparent. You could see the stock is up 7.64% today. We have a Strong Buy.

If you clicked on the ratings tab, it will go back and show you the history going back three years. And, basically, every single trading day over three years, you could see what the directional recommendation was. And you could also see what our grade is on valuation, growth, profitability, momentum, and analyst EPS revisions. And these are the same grades that you find on the right hand side.

So, underneath the rating summary card and that rating summary card actually shows three independent ratings. These aren't all quant. When you see SA Analysts, that is the consensus of the Seeking Alpha contributors and their recommendations. When you see Wall Street, that is a consensus of Wall Street analysts and their recommendation, and then we have our Quant system. And that Quant system, that Strong Buy grade is determined by the factor grades that you see underneath, the same factor grades that you found on the rating card. So, that rating card gives you a history going back three years.

If you're looking at the right hand rail here, this shows you what the grades are right now, but the beauty of this is, you not only get to see what it is relative to the rest of the sector. So, if you're looking at valuation, you see it's a C. It's pretty much in-line with the sector, but if you look at growth, you could see that instant characterization how growth is far, far stronger for Celestica versus the rest of the IT sector. Profitability is stronger. The momentum of the stock is stronger, and the analysts revisions are stronger as well.

But just to give you a little bit more color, not only do you see what it is now, you could see where the grades were three months and six months ago. And for the most part, Celestica has been fairly even. Valuation is basically unchanged going from D+ to C-. Growth has remained at an A-, which is much stronger than the rest of the group. Profitability has actually improved from B- to B+. So, as the company is earning more money, they're actually becoming more profitable, and the analyst revisions is a B+ versus an A. So, what does that mean? What makes up these factor grades?

If I actually click on it, if I click on valuation, you could see all the underlying metrics that make up this overall valuation grade. Now, I will tell you, not all metrics are created equal. Some of these have a higher weighting in our model. So, for instance, as you're looking at P/E versus price-to-sales versus price-to-book versus price-to-cash flow, we have back-tested each of these metrics, and we know which one has a greater predictive value in terms of what is beneficial to the stock in the future. So, then we created the PRO Quant Portfolio. And here, we're showing off a couple of the ideas too. This actually just started last June. And since last June, you could see it's up 29% versus the S&P on an equal weighted basis because this is an equal weighted portfolio. The S&P only up 10% for that period. And even looking year-to-date, it's up 3.58%, compared to the S&P on an equal weighted basis up 1.31%. S&P on a market cap weighted basis, obviously, down about 3% as of yesterday's closings. Today, these will be a lot higher on both sides. So, whether you're looking at all our Quant Strong Buys or these portfolio products that we're creating, the Quant does work really well.

DS: Steve, I know we're coming up at the top of hour. I'm going to start rapidly going through some of these questions as much as we can.

SC: Yeah. Please do.

DS: You had mentioned geopolitical risk that's going on in the market right now. Does the Quant system incorporate that geopolitical risk? Does it bring in news? Does it bring in technical analysis?

SC: So, as I mentioned, the model is both historical looking and forward looking. So, we're looking at valuation levels and growth rates from the past, but we also look at Wall Street consensus for revenue growth and for earnings growth and EBITDA growth. If a geopolitical event or any regulatory event, any type of macro event, if it's going to have an impact on the industry, many times analysts will change their numbers. We have what we call the revisions grade. So, indirectly, we bring those analyst revisions into our model.

So, when I click on the revisions grade here, you could see, for Celestica, obviously, despite all the geopolitical events that are happening, analysts think this company is going to continue to do better than they expected, so they're revising their numbers up. If a geopolitical event, say, maybe on a company that was very interest rate sensitive or maybe in the energy sector, if analysts thought the numbers would be going up or down, they would move their estimates, and we would capture it with a revision grade.

DS: So, talking about the revisions grade, there was a question of, is there a minimum number of analysts that need to be covering a stock for that grade to be calculated, or is it…

SC: Yes.

DS: …just minimum of one? Or, okay. What is it?

SC: One is it. We just need one analyst to be covering it. Yeah.

DS: Awesome. Alright. Continuing going on here. So, there was a couple of questions asking about there's a few grades, or sorry, there's a few stocks across our site where it seems like the Quant Rating grade changes multiple times either a week or a month. What would cause such high fluctuation in the grade to whipsaw back and forth?

SC: Yeah. Stocks trade thousands and thousands of times a day, from the time that the market opens. And even in pre-market or post-market, stocks are trading as well, let alone during the day. So, the Quant system is measuring basically every single trade in essence because we're looking at the price, and then at the end of the day, we take a closing price, and we factor that closing price into many metrics.

So, by a good example, if we look at valuation. Okay? And often, it's a valuation or EPS revisions that can be responsible for the stock's overall directional recommendation. So, I believe what this subscriber individual was talking about was a Quant grade going maybe from a Strong Buy to Buy to Hold of this valuation grade. And remember, it's not just on an absolute basis. We're comparing it to the rest of the sector.

So, the movement of the stock, the movement of the sector in conjunction with each other with all the trades that are occurring are, in essence, it's almost like a real time system. And if a stock begins to get overvalued versus a sector, and often we'll find, like, if a stock goes for – a stock could be a Strong Buy and have a value grade of a D, but if it becomes a D-, that's a circuit breaker. And when that circuit breaker is hit, it will drop the rating down from a Strong Buy to a Hold because a D- is just too low for valuation grade. And then a couple days later, the valuation could look a little bit different, so it may go back up.

So, it all depends how the stocks are trading and how they're trading versus the sector. And stocks trade, as I said, thousands of times a day. And when you measure it against all the trades for the sector, you're looking at millions and millions of trades. And our Quant system measures all of it.

DS: Yeah. And we update it every single day. Right?

SC: Every single day.

DS: Somebody asked about the specific time. It's 4:00 to 6:00 AM, I think, is what we've usually said. Is that right?

SC: That's right. From 4:00 to 6:00. And I know for some people that could be frustrating because they might use to, they'll look at an analyst report that was written maybe, like, a week or two weeks ago, and the analyst has a Strong Buy on it. And they're like, it's a good valuation. But the truth of the matter is, looking at something, like, two weeks ago, that's looking at, like, a newspaper article from two weeks ago. Do you really want to make your investment decision based on data that's two weeks old? For me, that doesn't work, and that's part of why I created the Quant system. I want to look, and I want to make investment decisions based on the data that's here right now in the moment.

DS: Yeah. Exactly. Somebody did ask, do we also refresh these on the weekends, or is it only on trading days?

SC: Well, it reflects all the trading days. So, on Saturday, it would update for Friday.

DS: Alright. Great. But no updates on Sunday, to clarify?

SC: No updates on Sunday. No.

DS: Great. And just to clarify as well, everybody, that is Eastern time zone when I was talking about 4:00 to 6:00 AM. Just so you're aware. There was a question. Are stock buybacks factored into the Quant model at all for these companies?

SC: I like stock buybacks. So, there are a number of metrics that we look at, and that could be one of the metrics that we look at. We don't show all the metrics that we look at.

DS: Oh, I see what you're saying…

SC: I'm actually glad that question came up, because if you're looking at the valuation page here or you're looking at the growth page here or the profitability page here, we give you a really close idea of how many metrics are out there for each of these investment characteristics, but there are a couple proprietary metrics that we don't show, and that's because we don't want our competitors to see it. So, what you look at gives you a really close intuitive idea. But I will say, Daniel, on occasion, sometimes it doesn't seem intuitive because it looks like something's a little bit off. And if it looks like it's a little bit off, it could be that it's one of the metrics that we don't display.

DS: Got it. Alright. I see what you're putting down there. Michael's been asking throughout the webinar, do we cover Canadian stocks with the Quant system?

SC: We have ADRs that we cover. And, yeah, I believe we do have some Canadian stocks as well. Did Michael give you an example of one Canadian stock?

DS: He did not.

SC: Okay.

DS: But maybe he'll chime in, in the chat here in a second. But…

SC: I see Royal Bank, I know, like, Royal Bank of Canada. They have an ADR. So…

DS: Shopify account?

SC: Yeah. So, that like, yeah, most of the ADRs will show up.

DS: ATD. Why don't you go ahead click that one in?

SC: ATD?

DS: Yeah.

SC: Alright. This is what I see for A as in Alpha, T as in Tomahawk, D as in Delta.

DS: Doesn't look like we get that one. Or Mark says…

SC: Oh, yeah. We do. See, ATD, just came up. So, if we click on that and we go to the summary page, you could see that we have the stock, but it doesn't have coverage from the Quant system. And it didn't look like there was an ADR in this company. So, there is information that you can get. So, what I do want to say is, if you click on, like, the growth tab here, okay, it will show you the absolute data points, but it's not going to show you those academic letter grades that you have with the Quant system because we're not comparing it to the rest of the sector.

So, we do have data for Canadian stocks, but it would be absolute data. If there is an ADR, you would have the Quant system in place. If there's no ADR for the Canadian stock, you would just have the raw data. So, we show the valuation. So, a lot of the metrics we look at are there, and you're just going to look at the absolute metric. You're not going to have the Quant grade.

DS: Alright. Got that clarified. Alright. Let's try to squeeze in one or two more here. Do we use any discount cash flow models within the Quant system?

SC: We do not. It's something that, historically, I have found ineffective, so we do not include that in our model. I know a lot of analysts do like to use it, but I think even in this article, I may address that. We do. We say, how is the value grade determined? Discounted cash flow models are highly sensitive to the company's assumed long-term growth rate. We don't know what the long-term growth rate is. So, it's one of the reasons why we don't use it. A small change in the company's assumed long-term growth rate can have a large impact on the resulting fair value for the stock.

Long-term growth rates are extremely hard to estimate with precision, and analysts have few tools other than guesswork for estimating them. So, that is one of the reasons that we do not use DCF. Amazing that he asked it, and, yeah, the answer is right there.

DS: Love it. Love it. How many years are included in forward metrics that we have across the grades?

SC: Alright. Let's go there. We're going to show you this. So, we'll go to Credo Technology. This is how transparent our system is. If we go to, let's say, well, let's just go to revenue forward growth. You could see, we actually have a tool tip, which displays the information how that metric works. And you could see for our forward growth rate, we're actually using one historical year and going forward two more years. So, it'd be one historical year. So, you have an actual in there. Then you have the current year, then you have FY1 and FY2. So, that's a total of four different periods that we use, and you would find the same for the EBITDA forward growth rate and for the EPS forward growth rate.

So, our growth rate, that calculation that we're using, it's a bit proprietary to Seeking Alpha, and it's one of the things that separates us. And as I have mentioned, I've been doing this for a long time. We've back-tested a lot of these metrics. So, I tend to find that using a longer growth rate period with including one historical year is actually a much more effective tool for measurement.

DS: Alright. There you have it. Alright, Steven Cress. Thank you so much for the time and the knowledge here today.

SC: Absolutely.

DS: This has been extremely helpful. Diving into the world of Quant. I know we've gotten a lot of questions from people over the many months and years, and we needed to re-dive in with everybody. And if you're watching the replay of this video, if you're new to Seeking Alpha Premium or PRO or you're joining in for Alpha Picks, hopefully, you found a lot of great insights here as well. I know it can seem a little bit intimidating at times, but I promise you it's not.

And, also, like I said, there's going to be another webinar with Steve here at the end of this month. There'll be another video on the replay at the Video Hub here on Seeking Alpha that'll dive into more of portfolio health check screeners and some of the other Quant features here. But Steve is, like I say, the mastermind, the Titan of Quant here at Seeking Alpha. It's always a pleasure to dive into this information with you. So, I know everybody's here saying, great. Thank you so much, Steve. Somebody's asking your age. I thought that's not what we do here guys.

SC: I've been in the business for a long time.

DS: We're here for the education.

SC: A lot of experience. A lot of experience.

DS: That's right. That's where all the hair went. Right? Alright, Steve. Let's hop on out of here. Everyone, thank you so much for joining us today. This has been very great, and we'll see you here again soon in the next video. Take care.

SC: Thanks so much. Bye-bye.
